import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)

]

monthly_dataframes = []

for file in file_list:
    df = pd.read_csv(file)
    monthly_dataframes.append(df)

ae_12_months = pd.concat(monthly_dataframes, ignore_index=True)

In [5]:
ae_clean = ae_12_months.copy()

ae_clean.columns = (
    ae_clean.columns
    .str.lower()
    .str.replace("&", "and", regex=False)
    .str.replace("+", "plus", regex=False)
    .str.replace("-", "_", regex=False)
    .str.replace("/", "_", regex=False)
    .str.replace(" ", "_", regex=False)
    .str.replace("__", "_", regex=False)
)

ae_clean.columns
Out[5]:
Index(['period', 'org_code', 'parent_org', 'org_name',
       'aande_attendances_type_1', 'aande_attendances_type_2',
       'aande_attendances_other_aande_department',
       'aande_attendances_booked_appointments_type_1',
       'aande_attendances_booked_appointments_type_2',
       'aande_attendances_booked_appointments_other_department',
       'attendances_over_4hrs_type_1', 'attendances_over_4hrs_type_2',
       'attendances_over_4hrs_other_department',
       'attendances_over_4hrs_booked_appointments_type_1',
       'attendances_over_4hrs_booked_appointments_type_2',
       'attendances_over_4hrs_booked_appointments_other_department',
       'patients_who_have_waited_4_12_hs_from_dta_to_admission',
       'patients_who_have_waited_12plus_hrs_from_dta_to_admission',

       'emergency_admissions_via_aande__type_2',
       'emergency_admissions_via_aande__other_aande_department',
       'other_emergency_admissions'],
      dtype='str')
In [6]:
ae_clean["total_ae_attendances"] = (
    ae_clean["aande_attendances_type_1"] +
    ae_clean["aande_attendances_type_2"] +
    ae_clean["aande_attendances_other_aande_department"]
)

ae_clean["total_attendances_over_4hrs"] = (
    ae_clean["attendances_over_4hrs_type_1"] +
    ae_clean["attendances_over_4hrs_type_2"] +
    ae_clean["attendances_over_4hrs_other_department"]
)

ae_clean["four_hour_breach_rate"] = (
    ae_clean["total_attendances_over_4hrs"] / ae_clean["total_ae_attendances"]
) * 100

ae_clean["four_hour_performance_rate"] = 100 - ae_clean["four_hour_breach_rate"]

ae_clean[[
    "period",
    "org_name",
    "total_ae_attendances",
    "total_attendances_over_4hrs",
    "four_hour_breach_rate",
    "four_hour_performance_rate"
]].head()

In [8]:
ae_clean["period_clean"] = (
    ae_clean["period_clean"]
    .astype(str)
    .str.strip()
    .str.upper()
)

ae_clean = ae_clean[ae_clean["period_clean"] != "TOTAL"]

ae_clean["period_date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    ae_clean["period_clean"],
    format="%B-%Y"
)

ae_clean[["period", "period_clean", "period_date"]].head()

In [9]:
ae_clean["period_month"] = ae_clean["period_date"].dt.strftime("%b %Y")

monthly_summary = ae_clean.groupby("period_date").agg({
    "total_ae_attendances": "sum",
    "total_attendances_over_4hrs": "sum"
}).reset_index()

monthly_summary["four_hour_breach_rate"] = (
    monthly_summary["total_attendances_over_4hrs"] / monthly_summary["total_ae_attendances"]
) * 100

monthly_summary["four_hour_performance_rate"] = 100 - monthly_summary["four_hour_breach_rate"]

monthly_summary["period_month"] = monthly_summary["period_date"].dt.strftime("%b %Y")

monthly_summary

plt.plot(
    monthly_summary["period_month"],
    monthly_summary["four_hour_breach_rate"],
    marker="o"
)

plt.axhline(
    y=5,
    linestyle="--",
    label="5% breach threshold"
)

plt.xlabel("Month")
plt.ylabel("4-hour breach rate (%)")
plt.title("Monthly A&E 4-hour Breach Rate, Apr 2025 to Mar 2026")

plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.legend()
plt.show()
In [13]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

plt.plot(
    monthly_summary["period_month"],
    monthly_summary["four_hour_performance_rate"],
    marker="o"
)

plt.axhline(
    y=95,
    linestyle="--",
    label="95% constitutional standard"
)

plt.axhline(
    y=78,
    linestyle="--",
    label="78% recovery target"
)

plt.xlabel("Month")
plt.ylabel("4-hour performance rate (%)")
plt.title("Monthly A&E 4-hour Performance Rate, Apr 2025 to Mar 2026")

plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.legend()
plt.show()

Initial interpretation: 12-month A&E performance trend¶

This chart shows the monthly A&E 4-hour performance rate from April 2025 to March 2026.

The performance rate represents the percentage of A&E attendances that were not recorded as waiting over 4 hours. A higher value indicates better performance, while a lower value suggests greater pressure on emergency care services.

The 95% reference line represents the historical constitutional standard for A&E 4-hour performance. The 78% reference line provides a more recent recovery benchmark used in operational planning. Comparing the monthly trend against both lines helps show how far current performance is from long-term expectations and recent recovery aims.

This trend analysis is useful because NHS England publishes A&E attendances and emergency admissions monthly, including attendances across A&E types, 4-hour performance and waits after decision to admit.

In [14]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

plt.plot(
    monthly_summary["period_month"],
    monthly_summary["total_ae_attendances"],
    marker="o"
)

plt.xlabel("Month")
plt.ylabel("Total A&E attendances")
plt.title("Monthly A&E Attendances, Apr 2025 to Mar 2026")

plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

Monthly A&E attendance trend¶

This chart shows total A&E attendances by month from April 2025 to March 2026.

This helps show whether changes in 4-hour performance may be linked to changes in demand. If attendances rise while performance falls, this may suggest that higher demand is contributing to pressure on emergency care services.

This analysis uses monthly NHS England A&E data across all A&E department types.

In [15]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

plt.scatter(
    monthly_summary["total_ae_attendances"],
    monthly_summary["four_hour_performance_rate"]
)

plt.xlabel("Total A&E attendances")
plt.ylabel("4-hour performance rate (%)")
plt.title("Relationship Between A&E Attendances and 4-hour Performance")

plt.show()

Relationship between A&E attendances and 4-hour performance¶

This scatter plot compares total monthly A&E attendances with the 4-hour performance rate.

Each point represents one month. The purpose is to explore whether months with higher A&E demand also show lower 4-hour performance.

This does not prove that higher attendances directly caused lower performance, because A&E waiting times can also be affected by staffing levels, bed availability, discharge delays, case complexity and wider hospital flow. However, it provides an early view of whether demand pressure may be linked to performance changes.

In [16]:
attendance_performance_correlation = monthly_summary[
    ["total_ae_attendances", "four_hour_performance_rate"]
].corr()

attendance_performance_correlation
Out[16]:
total_ae_attendances four_hour_performance_rate
total_ae_attendances 1.000000 -0.165822
four_hour_performance_rate -0.165822 1.000000

Correlation between A&E attendances and 4-hour performance¶

The correlation between total monthly A&E attendances and 4-hour performance rate was -0.17.

This indicates a weak negative relationship. In this dataset, months with higher total A&E attendances were slightly associated with lower 4-hour performance, but the relationship was not strong.

This means demand may be one factor linked to performance pressure, but it is unlikely to explain the full picture on its own. Other operational factors such as staffing, bed availability, discharge delays, case mix and wider hospital flow may also affect A&E waiting times.

For this reason, the result should be treated as exploratory rather than causal.

In [17]:
provider_summary = ae_clean.groupby("org_name").agg({
    "total_ae_attendances": "sum",
    "total_attendances_over_4hrs": "sum"
}).reset_index()

provider_summary["average_four_hour_breach_rate"] = (
    provider_summary["total_attendances_over_4hrs"] / provider_summary["total_ae_attendances"]
) * 100

provider_summary["average_four_hour_performance_rate"] = (
    100 - provider_summary["average_four_hour_breach_rate"]
)

provider_summary = provider_summary.sort_values(
    "average_four_hour_performance_rate",
    ascending=True
)

provider_summary.head(10)

In [18]:
bottom_10_providers = provider_summary.head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

plt.barh(
    bottom_10_providers["org_name"],
    bottom_10_providers["average_four_hour_performance_rate"]
)

plt.xlabel("Average 4-hour performance rate (%)")
plt.ylabel("Provider organisation")
plt.title("10 Providers with Lowest Average A&E 4-hour Performance, Apr 2025 to Mar 2026")

plt.gca().invert_yaxis()

plt.show()

Providers with the lowest average 4-hour performance¶

This chart shows the 10 provider organisations with the lowest average A&E 4-hour performance rate across the 12-month period.

The calculation uses total attendances and total attendances over 4 hours across the full year, rather than looking at a single month. This gives a more stable view of provider-level performance.

This analysis should be interpreted carefully. Lower performance may reflect several factors, including emergency demand, acuity, bed availability, discharge delays, staffing, and wider patient flow across the hospital. The chart identifies where performance pressure appears highest, but it does not explain the cause on its own.

bottom_10_filtered_providers = provider_summary_filtered.head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

plt.barh(
    bottom_10_filtered_providers["org_name"],
    bottom_10_filtered_providers["average_four_hour_performance_rate"]
)

plt.xlabel("Average 4-hour performance rate (%)")
plt.ylabel("Provider organisation")
plt.title("10 Larger Providers with Lowest Average A&E 4-hour Performance, Apr 2025 to Mar 2026")

plt.gca().invert_yaxis()

plt.show()

Larger providers with the lowest average 4-hour performance¶

This chart shows the 10 larger provider organisations with the lowest average A&E 4-hour performance rate across the 12-month period.

A minimum attendance threshold of 50,000 attendances across the year was used to reduce the effect of very small providers. This makes the comparison more meaningful because very small services can show extreme percentages due to lower activity volumes.

The results should still be interpreted carefully. Lower performance may reflect a combination of demand, acuity, bed availability, discharge delays, staffing levels and wider hospital flow.

In [24]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

plt.plot(
    monthly_dta_summary["period_month"],
    monthly_dta_summary["patients_who_have_waited_4_12_hs_from_dta_to_admission"],
    marker="o",
    label="4-12 hour DTA waits"
)

plt.plot(
    monthly_dta_summary["period_month"],
    monthly_dta_summary["patients_who_have_waited_12plus_hrs_from_dta_to_admission"],
    marker="o",
    label="12+ hour DTA waits"
)

plt.xlabel("Month")
plt.ylabel("Number of patients")
plt.title("Monthly Decision-to-Admit Waits, Apr 2025 to Mar 2026")

plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.legend()
plt.show()

Monthly decision-to-admit waits¶

This chart shows monthly decision-to-admit waits from April 2025 to March 2026.

Decision-to-admit waits measure patients who had already been assessed and had a decision made to admit them, but were still waiting for admission to hospital. This is different from the overall A&E 4-hour performance measure because it focuses specifically on admitted patients waiting after the decision to admit.

The chart separates 4-12 hour waits and 12+ hour waits. The 12+ hour waits are particularly important because they may indicate severe pressure in hospital flow, bed availability and discharge capacity.

This analysis is useful because it moves beyond overall A&E attendance numbers and looks at a more specific patient-flow pressure point.

In [25]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

plt.plot(
    monthly_dta_summary["period_month"],
    monthly_dta_summary["total_dta_waits_over_4hrs"],
    marker="o"
)

plt.xlabel("Month")
plt.ylabel("Total DTA waits over 4 hours")
plt.title("Monthly Total Decision-to-Admit Waits Over 4 Hours, Apr 2025 to Mar 2026")

plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

Total decision-to-admit waits over 4 hours¶

This chart shows the total number of patients who waited more than 4 hours after a decision had been made to admit them.

This is an important patient-flow measure because these patients had already been assessed and needed admission, but were still waiting for a bed or suitable admission pathway.

A rising number of DTA waits may suggest pressure beyond the emergency department itself, including bed availability, discharge capacity, inpatient flow and wider hospital demand.

This does not prove the exact cause of the waits, but it helps identify months where admitted-patient flow pressure was higher.

In [26]:
provider_dta_summary = ae_clean.groupby("org_name").agg({
    "patients_who_have_waited_4_12_hs_from_dta_to_admission": "sum",
    "patients_who_have_waited_12plus_hrs_from_dta_to_admission": "sum",
    "total_dta_waits_over_4hrs": "sum",
    "total_ae_attendances": "sum"
}).reset_index()

provider_dta_summary = provider_dta_summary[
    provider_dta_summary["org_name"] != "TOTAL"
]

provider_dta_summary = provider_dta_summary.sort_values(
    "patients_who_have_waited_12plus_hrs_from_dta_to_admission",
    ascending=False
)

top_10_dta_12hr_providers = provider_dta_summary.head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

plt.barh(
    top_10_dta_12hr_providers["org_name"],
    top_10_dta_12hr_providers["patients_who_have_waited_12plus_hrs_from_dta_to_admission"]
)

plt.xlabel("12+ hour DTA waits")
plt.ylabel("Provider organisation")
plt.title("10 Providers with Highest 12+ Hour DTA Waits, Apr 2025 to Mar 2026")

plt.gca().invert_yaxis()

plt.show()

Providers with the highest 12+ hour decision-to-admit waits¶

This chart shows the 10 provider organisations with the highest number of 12+ hour decision-to-admit waits across the 12-month period.

Decision-to-admit waits measure patients who had already been assessed and had a decision made to admit them, but were still waiting for admission. The 12+ hour category is particularly important because it indicates more severe admitted-patient flow pressure.

This chart uses absolute patient counts, so larger providers may naturally appear higher. The results should be interpreted alongside attendance volume, hospital size, bed availability, discharge capacity and wider system pressure.

In [28]:
provider_dta_summary["dta_12hr_waits_per_1000_attendances"] = (
    provider_dta_summary["patients_who_have_waited_12plus_hrs_from_dta_to_admission"] /
    provider_dta_summary["total_ae_attendances"]
) * 1000

provider_dta_summary_rate = provider_dta_summary[
    provider_dta_summary["total_ae_attendances"] >= 50000
].sort_values(
    "dta_12hr_waits_per_1000_attendances",
    ascending=False
)

top_10_dta_rate_providers = provider_dta_summary_rate.head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

plt.barh(
    top_10_dta_rate_providers["org_name"],
    top_10_dta_rate_providers["dta_12hr_waits_per_1000_attendances"]
)

plt.xlabel("12+ hour DTA waits per 1,000 A&E attendances")
plt.ylabel("Provider organisation")
plt.title("10 Providers with Highest 12+ Hour DTA Wait Rate, Apr 2025 to Mar 2026")

plt.gca().invert_yaxis()

plt.show()

Providers with the highest 12+ hour DTA wait rate¶

This chart shows the 10 larger provider organisations with the highest rate of 12+ hour decision-to-admit waits per 1,000 A&E attendances.

This is a fairer comparison than using raw counts alone because it accounts for provider size. A large hospital may naturally have more 12+ hour DTA waits simply because it sees more patients. By calculating the rate per 1,000 attendances, we can identify providers where long DTA waits appear high relative to overall A&E activity.

A minimum threshold of 50,000 attendances across the year was used to reduce distortion from very small providers.

This measure should still be interpreted carefully. High rates may reflect bed availability, discharge delays, patient acuity, staffing levels, local demand, or wider system flow challenges.
